findings.description: Expression Language Injection via buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate with user-controlled exception message containing the cron expression
findings.code_snippet: context.buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate(e.getMessage()).addConstraintViolation();
findings.reasoning: The CronValidator.isValid() method catches IllegalArgumentException from CronParser.parse(value), where value is the user-supplied cron string. The exception message directly embeds the user input (e.g., in CronParser line 131: String.format("Failed to parse '%s'. %s", expression, e.getMessage())). This tainted exception message is then passed directly to context.buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate(), which in Bean Validation (Hibernate Validator implementation) evaluates the string as an EL expression. An attacker can embed EL expressions like ${Runtime.getRuntime().exec('cmd')} in the cron string, causing them to be executed when the constraint violation message template is evaluated. This is the same pattern as CVE-2014-0114 affecting many Bean Validation implementations using Hibernate Validator.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: User input (cron string) -> CronParser.parse(expression) at line 29 of CronValidator -> exception thrown with user input embedded in message (CronParser.java line 131) -> exception caught -> e.getMessage() passed to buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate() at CronValidator line 33 -> EL evaluation of user-controlled string.
evidence.notes: The Hibernate Validator implementation of buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate() evaluates the template string as an EL expression before rendering it. This means ${...} and #{...} expressions are evaluated. An attacker can inject EL expressions like ${Runtime.getRuntime().exec(new String[]{'/bin/bash','-c','id'})} to achieve RCE. The fix is to use a literal constraint message instead of relying on the exception message, or to escape the message before passing it.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker submits a cron expression containing EL syntax like ${Runtime.getRuntime().exec('id')} to any endpoint that uses the @Cron annotation for validation, causing arbitrary OS command execution on the server
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Application must use the @Cron Bean Validation annotation with Hibernate Validator as the Bean Validation provider (which is the default in Spring Boot and most Java EE applications)
exploit_payload.category: Remote code execution via Expression Language injection

findings.description: User input included in exception message - not a code injection vulnerability
findings.reasoning: This throws an IllegalArgumentException with user-supplied cron expression content in the message. There is no code execution, script evaluation, deserialization, JNDI lookup, or expression language injection. The user input is simply reflected in an exception message string. This does not match any of the relevant CWEs (CWE-94, CWE-95, CWE-917, CWE-502, CWE-470). At most, if the exception message is logged, this could represent a log injection risk, but that is not within scope of this review group.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: User input flows into FieldExpression.asString() which is then used in String.format() to construct an exception message. No code execution path exists.
evidence.notes: Not a code injection vulnerability. Simple string concatenation in exception message.

findings.description: User input included in exception message - not a code injection vulnerability
findings.code_snippet: throw new IllegalArgumentException(String.format("Invalid range! [%s,%s]", from, to));
findings.reasoning: Exception message includes integer values derived from user input (from/to range values parsed from cron expression). No code execution path. The values are integers, not arbitrary strings, further reducing any risk. Not a code injection vulnerability per the relevant CWEs.

evidence.notes: Values are integers cast from user input, not raw strings. No code execution path.

findings.description: Hardcoded string in exception message - no user input involved
findings.code_snippet: throw new IllegalArgumentException("No special characters allowed in range, except for 'L'");
findings.reasoning: This exception uses a hardcoded string message with no user input at all. The CodeQL finding message itself notes the arg is the hardcoded string "No special characters allowed in range, except for 'L'". This is entirely safe - no user data is in the exception message.

evidence.notes: Hardcoded message with no user input. Clearly a false positive.

findings.description: User input (integer value) included in exception message - not a code injection vulnerability
findings.code_snippet: throw new IllegalArgumentException(String.format(OORANGE, value, constraints.getStartRange(), constraints.getEndRange()));
findings.reasoning: Exception message includes an integer value derived from user input. The value is already parsed as an int (not a raw string), so there is no log injection risk either. No code execution path. Not a code injection vulnerability per the relevant CWEs.

evidence.notes: Value is an integer, not a raw string. No code execution path.

findings.description: User input (integer value) included in exception message - not a code injection vulnerability
findings.code_snippet:
throw new IllegalArgumentException(
        String.format("Period %s not in range [%s, %s]", value, constraints.getStartRange(), constraints.getEndRange()));
findings.reasoning: Exception message includes an integer value derived from user input (already parsed as int). No code execution path. Not a code injection vulnerability per the relevant CWEs.

evidence.notes: Value is an integer. No code execution path.

findings.description: Hardcoded string in exception message - no user input involved
findings.code_snippet: throw new IllegalArgumentException("Empty expression!");
findings.reasoning: This exception uses a completely hardcoded string message with no user input. The CodeQL finding message itself states the arg is the hardcoded literal "Empty expression!". This is entirely safe and a clear false positive.

evidence.notes: Hardcoded message, no user input in exception message.

findings.description: User input included in exception message - not a code injection vulnerability
findings.code_snippet: throw new IllegalArgumentException(String.format("Invalid field value! Trailing commas not permitted! '%s'", fieldWithTrailingCommas));
findings.reasoning: Exception message includes a user-supplied cron field value. No code execution path exists. Simply reflects the invalid user input in the error message. Not a code injection vulnerability per the relevant CWEs.

evidence.notes: User input in exception message. No code execution path.

findings.description: User input included in exception message - not a code injection vulnerability
findings.code_snippet:
throw new IllegalArgumentException(
        String.format("Cron expression contains %s parts but we expect one of %s", expressionLength, expressions.keySet()));
findings.reasoning: Exception message includes the count of parts in the user-supplied cron expression (an integer) and the set of expected part counts (server-side constant). No raw user string is directly reflected and no code execution path exists. Not a code injection vulnerability.

evidence.notes: expressionLength is an integer derived from splitting user input. expressions.keySet() is server-side. No code execution path.

findings.description: User input included in exception message - not a code injection vulnerability
findings.code_snippet: throw new IllegalArgumentException(String.format("Failed to parse '%s'. %s", expression, e.getMessage()), e);
findings.reasoning: Exception message wraps both the user-supplied expression string and the inner exception message. No code execution path exists. This is a standard error reporting pattern. Not a code injection vulnerability per the relevant CWEs (CWE-94, CWE-95, CWE-917, CWE-502, CWE-470).

evidence.notes: User input reflected in wrapping exception message. No code execution path.
